---
title: "Module 10: Corporate Agent Deployment"
subtitle: "MGMT 675: Generative AI for Finance"
author: "Kerry Back, Rice University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, revealjs-style.scss]
    slide-number: c
    transition: fade
    navigation-mode: linear
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    margin: 0.05
    center: true
    include-after-body:
      - text: |
          <script src="https://hypothes.is/embed.js" async></script>
---

## Guardrails and Constraints {.section-divider}

## Why Guardrails?

Without constraints, an autonomous agent can cause real damage --- even with good intentions.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-dark}
[**What Can Go Wrong**]{.card-title}

- *"Clean up the repo"* → deletes files you need
- A loop bug triggers hundreds of API calls
- Write access to the database → unintended edits
- A misread instruction → wrong files overwritten
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**The Principle**]{.card-title}

Grant the agent the [minimum access]{.amber} it needs to complete the task --- and no more.

- Read-only unless writes are required
- Specific directories, not the whole filesystem
- No network unless the task requires it
:::
:::

## Permission Modes in Claude Code

Claude Code pauses and asks before taking consequential actions --- unless you explicitly allow them.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Default: Human in the Loop**

- Agent stops before running bash commands, writing files, or calling the network
- You approve or deny each action
- Safe for unfamiliar tasks or new codebases
::::

::::{.column}
**Allow Lists**

- Approve a specific command once --- or always
- Approve an entire tool (e.g., all file reads)
- Approve a path prefix (e.g., `./reports/`)
- Stored in `settings.json`
::::
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
[Human approval is the default.]{.amber} You choose how much autonomy to grant, task by task.
:::

## CLAUDE.md as a Behavioral Guardrail

CLAUDE.md is not just documentation --- it is the agent's system prompt. Use it to enforce rules.

:::{.info-box}
[**Examples of Constraint Instructions**]{.box-title}

```markdown
- Never modify files outside the src/ directory
- Always ask before running git push or git commit
- Use read-only database connections unless told otherwise
- If a task would delete more than 3 files, pause and confirm
```
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
The system prompt is the [first line of defense]{.amber}. Clear written rules reduce the chance of unintended actions.
:::

## Hooks: Programmatic Checks

Hooks run shell commands automatically at agent lifecycle events.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Event Types**]{.card-title}

- **PreToolUse** --- runs before a tool fires; can block the action
- **PostToolUse** --- runs after a tool completes; can log or validate
- **Stop** --- runs when the agent finishes
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Example Uses**]{.card-title}

- Block any bash command containing `rm -rf`
- Log every file write to an audit trail
- Send an alert when the agent finishes
- Run tests automatically after every code edit
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
Hooks enforce rules [programmatically]{.amber} --- the check runs whether or not the agent remembers the instruction.
:::

## Layered Guardrails

No single guardrail is sufficient. Defense in depth combines all layers.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
1. **CLAUDE.md** --- behavioral rules in the system prompt
2. **Permissions** --- tool and path access control
3. **Hooks** --- programmatic checks at runtime
::::

::::{.column}
4. **Sandboxing** --- isolated execution environment
5. **Human approval** --- pause before high-stakes actions
6. **Iteration limits** --- prevent runaway loops and cost overruns
::::
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:1em; text-align:center;"}
Each layer catches what the previous one misses. [Together they make autonomous agents safe to deploy.]{.amber}
:::

## Audit Logging {.section-divider}

## JSONL Audit Logs

Every AI interaction in a production system should produce an [audit log entry]{.amber}. The standard format is JSONL (one JSON object per line).

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**What to Log**]{.card-title}

- **Identity**: timestamp, user, model
- **Content**: prompt and response
- **Metadata**: token count, latency, cost
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Why It Matters**]{.card-title}

- **Compliance**: Regulators can audit what the AI said
- **Debugging**: Trace errors back to the exact prompt
- **Cost and quality**: Track token usage and identify common failures for skill updates
:::
:::

Store logs locally or in a cloud database. [If it's not logged, it didn't happen.]{.amber}

## Three Layers of Auditability {.section-divider}

## Defense in Depth

:::{.step-flow}
:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
📄
:::
:::{.step-title}
Layer 1
:::
:::{.step-desc}
RAG Citations
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
💻
:::
:::{.step-title}
Layer 2
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Code Transparency
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
📋
:::
:::{.step-title}
Layer 3
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Audit Logs
:::
:::
:::

1. **RAG citations**: Ground answers in source documents with traceable references. AI answers based on [your content]{.amber}, not its training data.

2. **Code transparency**: AI displays the SQL or Python it used. Analysts review the logic, not just the answer.

3. **Audit logs**: Every prompt, response, and token count recorded in JSONL. Complete trail of every AI decision.

[The more layers, the harder it is for errors to reach your team.]{.amber}

## Security and Data Policies {.section-divider}

## Data Policies: Training and Retention

Before sending data to any AI provider, know the answers to two questions.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Will they train on your data?**

- [Consumer tiers]{.amber} (free ChatGPT, free Claude) may use your input to improve models
- [API and enterprise tiers]{.amber} typically do not
- Check your agreement and settings
::::

::::{.column}
**How long do they keep it?**

- Providers retain prompts for a window (often 30 days) for safety monitoring
- Enterprise contracts may offer zero retention
- Know the policy [before]{.amber} you send sensitive data
::::
:::

## The Schema-Only Pattern

AI can analyze sensitive data [without ever reading it]{.amber}. It only needs the table structure.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 16
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '28px'}, 'flowchart': {'nodeSpacing': 80, 'rankSpacing': 120, 'padding': 24, 'useMaxWidth': true}}}%%
flowchart LR
  S["<b>Schema</b><br/><i>table names, columns</i>"] -->|"safe to share"| AI["<b>AI</b>"]
  AI --> SQL["<b>SQL Code</b>"]
  SQL --> L["<b>Runs Locally</b>"]
  DB["<b>Your Database</b>"] -->|"data stays here"| L

  style S fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
  style AI fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
  style SQL fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
  style L fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
  style DB fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
```

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**How it works**

1. Describe tables in instructions: names, columns, types
2. Ask AI to write the query
3. AI writes SQL from the schema alone; the script runs locally and output stays local
::::

::::{.column}
**Why it's compliant**

- Table metadata is [not PII]{.amber} --- safe to share
- Sensitive records stay on your server
- AI produces the *tool*, not the *output*
- Works for FERPA, HIPAA, SOX, GDPR
::::
:::

## Improving SQL Quality: Schema + Synthetic Rows

Schema alone works, but adding [~3 synthetic rows per table]{.amber} makes the generated SQL significantly more accurate.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Why sample rows help**

- Reveal data formats (is status `'Active'` or `1`?)
- Disambiguate columns (is `code` a country code or product code?)
- Help the LLM write correct WHERE clauses with properly formatted literals
::::

::::{.column}
**Rules**

- Rows are [synthetic]{.amber} --- fabricated to match real formats without containing actual records
- Three rows per table is the sweet spot; more tends to distract the model
- Databricks: ~3 rows per table raises SQL accuracy by [~6 pp]{.amber}
::::
:::

## Where Should AI Infrastructure Live?

When you connect an AI agent to enterprise data, you face a fundamental question: [where does the computation happen?]{.amber}

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Public Cloud AI**]{.card-title}

- Data sent to provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google)
- Fastest to deploy
- Provider controls infrastructure
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Private / On-Prem AI**]{.card-title}

- Data never leaves your network
- Full control over access
- Higher upfront cost
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Hybrid**]{.card-title}

- Sensitive data stays on-prem
- Non-sensitive tasks use cloud AI
- Most common enterprise pattern
:::
:::

Antonio Neri, CEO of HPE: "In the AI era, where data sovereignty, privacy and control of data matter, the industry is seeing a shift back to private clouds."

## Data Sovereignty

**Data sovereignty** means controlling where your data lives, who can access it, and what jurisdiction governs it.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Why it matters for AI**

- AI agents may transmit query results to external LLMs
- Regulations (GDPR, HIPAA, SOX) may restrict where data can go
- Defense agencies require air-gapped environments
::::

::::{.column}
**Enterprise examples**

- **DISA** (U.S. DoD): $931M on-prem private cloud
- **Barclays**: 50K+ workloads to private cloud
- **2degrees** (NZ): sovereign AI for data residency
::::
:::

## Data Security: Running AI Locally {.section-divider}

## The Case for Local AI

Cloud AI is convenient, but [local models]{.amber} keep data inside your machine --- no transmission, no retention policy, no provider to trust.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**When local AI makes sense**

- Client data under NDA
- HIPAA, GDPR, or FERPA-covered records
- Proprietary deal models and memos
- Regulated industries (banking, defense, healthcare)
::::

::::{.column}
**The trade-off**

| | Cloud AI | Local AI |
|---|---|---|
| Model quality | ★★★★★ | ★★★☆☆ |
| Data privacy | Provider policy | 100% local |
| Setup cost | None | Minutes |
| Compute | Unlimited | Your hardware |

::::
:::

[For narrow, sensitive tasks, a smaller local model is often the right call.]{.amber}

## Ollama: Local LLMs in One Installer

[Ollama](https://ollama.com) lets you run open-source models on your laptop --- no cloud, no API key, no data leaving your machine.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**How it works**

1. Download and run the Ollama installer
2. Pull a model (e.g., `llama3.2`)
3. Chat locally via a browser UI or the terminal
4. All inference runs on your CPU/GPU
::::

::::{.column}
**Available models**

- **Llama 3.1** (Meta, 8B) --- strong reasoning
- **Llama 3.2** (Meta, 3B) --- faster, lighter
- **Mistral**, **Gemma**, **Phi** --- many options
- Models download once and run offline
::::
:::

## Exercise: Install Ollama and Try Local LLMs {.section-divider}

## Exercise: Running a Local Model

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Setup**

1. Go to [ollama.com](https://ollama.com) and download the installer for your OS
2. Run the installer (no terminal needed)
3. Once installed, open a terminal and run:
   ```
   ollama run llama3.2
   ```
4. Then try:
   ```
   ollama run llama3.1
   ```
::::

::::{.column}
**The prompt**

Give each model this prompt and compare their responses:

> *"A private equity firm is evaluating a manufacturing company with $50M in revenue, 12% EBITDA margins, and 8% annual revenue growth. Comparable transactions have been done at 7--9x EBITDA. What is a reasonable valuation range, and what are the top three risks you would flag before proceeding?"*

**What to notice**: quality of reasoning, how each model handles uncertainty, and whether either refuses or hedges differently.
::::
:::

[Both models run entirely on your machine. No data is sent anywhere.]{.amber}

## ERP and the SaaSpocalypse {.section-divider}

## February 2026: $2 Trillion Vanished

In a single week, AI agents wiped [$2 trillion]{.amber} from enterprise software valuations.

:::{.stat-cards}
:::{.stat-card}
:::{.stat-number}
$2T
:::
:::{.stat-label}
Market cap erased from SaaS companies
:::
:::

:::{.stat-card}
:::{.stat-number}
30--50%
:::
:::{.stat-label}
Decline in leading ERP/CRM stocks
:::
:::

:::{.stat-card}
:::{.stat-number}
$250B+
:::
:::{.stat-label}
Annual enterprise software spend at risk
:::
:::
:::

The market's thesis: AI agents can [replace entire categories]{.amber} of enterprise software --- BI dashboards, reporting layers, workflow tools, Tier 1 support.

## What Investors Are Betting On

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-dark}
[**The Old Model**]{.card-title}

- Buy a CRM ($150/user/month)
- Buy a BI tool ($70/user/month)
- Buy an integration platform ($50K+/year)
- Buy a reporting tool ($30/user/month)
- Hire analysts to run queries and build dashboards
- Wait 3 weeks for a cross-system report
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**The AI Model**]{.card-title}

- One AI agent queries all your systems
- Natural language --- no SQL, no dashboard builder
- Cross-system analysis in seconds, not weeks
- Reports, charts, and narratives generated on demand
- The "last mile" from data to decision collapses
- [Cost per query: $0.01--0.05]{.amber}
:::
:::

## {.quote-slide}

## {.quote-slide}

:::{.quote-text}
"Every application that is essentially a [natural-language interface to a database]{.amber} is at risk. That's CRM, ERP reporting, BI dashboards, HR analytics, support ticket routing --- a huge fraction of enterprise software revenue."
:::

:::{.quote-source}
--- Investor thesis, widely circulated, February 2026
:::

## The Traditional Enterprise Stack {.section-divider}

## How Companies Run Today

A typical mid-market company ($500M revenue) runs [10+ enterprise systems]{.amber} across divisions.

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**CRM**]{.card-title}

- Salesforce, HubSpot, or legacy
- Pipeline, contacts, deals
- Each division may use a different one
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Finance / ERP**]{.card-title}

- NetSuite, SAP, QuickBooks
- GL, invoices, budgets
- Often different systems per division
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Operations & HR**]{.card-title}

- SAP, Oracle SCM, Workday
- Supply chain, inventory, headcount
- Typically centralized
:::
:::

These systems don't talk to each other. Getting a cross-system answer requires [ETL pipelines, data warehouses, or analyst time]{.amber}.

## The Cross-System Problem

A CEO asks: [*"Which customers buy from multiple divisions?"*]{.amber}

:::{.step-flow}
:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
1
:::
:::{.step-title}
Request
:::
:::{.step-desc}
CEO emails the BI team
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
2
:::
:::{.step-title}
Gather
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Analyst queries 3 CRMs separately
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
3
:::
:::{.step-title}
Reconcile
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Match "GE" / "GE Industrial" / "GE Safety"
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
4
:::
:::{.step-title}
Deliver
:::
:::{.step-desc}
3 weeks later: a spreadsheet
:::
:::
:::

The traditional answer is a [data warehouse]{.amber} ($500K--$5M, 6--18 months to build). The AI alternative: query each system where it lives and merge on the fly.

## Demo: Meridian Corp {.section-divider}

## Meridian Corp: A Simulated Enterprise

Meridian Corp is a fictional [$500M B2B industrial supplies distributor]{.amber} with three operating divisions and 10 enterprise systems.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Industrial Division** (~$250M)

- Salesforce CRM
- NetSuite Finance
- SAP Operations

**Energy Division** (~$150M)

- Legacy CRM (custom-built)
- QuickBooks Finance
- Oracle SCM
::::

::::{.column}
**Safety Division** (~$100M)

- HubSpot CRM
- Shared NetSuite Finance

**Corporate HQ**

- Workday HR (all divisions)
- NetSuite Consolidation
- Zendesk Support
::::
:::

[41 tables, 26,000+ rows, 3 years of data (2023--2025).]{.amber}

## Realistic Data Friction

The simulation includes the same data quality problems real enterprises face.

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Naming Conflicts**]{.card-title}

- Salesforce: `CamelCase` columns
- Legacy CRM: `snake_case` columns
- Same customer, different names across divisions
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Format Mismatches**]{.card-title}

- Legacy CRM stores dates as text: `"03/15/2025"`
- Other systems use proper date types
- Revenue in different currencies or fiscal calendars
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**No Cross-System Joins**]{.card-title}

- The agent cannot join across systems in SQL
- Must query each system separately
- Merges data with Python + fuzzy matching
:::
:::

This is [exactly]{.amber} how a real enterprise AI tool would need to work.

## The AI Agent Architecture

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 16
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '28px'}, 'flowchart': {'nodeSpacing': 80, 'rankSpacing': 120, 'padding': 24, 'useMaxWidth': true}}}%%
flowchart LR
  U["<b>User Question</b><br/><i>plain English</i>"] --> A["<b>AI Agent</b><br/><i>Claude</i>"]
  A --> Q1["<b>Salesforce</b>"]
  A --> Q2["<b>Legacy CRM</b>"]
  A --> Q3["<b>Workday</b>"]
  Q1 --> M["<b>Merge & Analyze</b><br/><i>Python</i>"]
  Q2 --> M
  Q3 --> M
  M --> R["<b>Answer</b><br/><i>charts + narrative</i>"]

  style U fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
  style A fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
  style Q1 fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
  style Q2 fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
  style Q3 fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
  style M fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
  style R fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
```

The agent writes SQL for each system, executes it, then uses Python to merge, reconcile, and visualize the combined results.

## Live Demo: Single-System Queries {.section-divider}

## Demo: Querying Individual Systems

We'll ask Meridian questions that hit [one system at a time]{.amber} --- the kind of query a dashboard or BI tool handles today.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Sales pipeline**

- "How many open deals are in Salesforce?"
- "Which sales rep has the highest win rate this quarter?"
- "What's the Energy Division's pipeline value?"
::::

::::{.column}
**Finance and HR**

- "What was Q4 revenue by division in NetSuite?"
- "How many employees does each division have?"
- "What's the average support ticket resolution time?"
::::
:::

Watch for: the SQL the agent writes, the speed of response, and the chart/narrative it generates.

## Live Demo: Cross-System Queries {.section-divider}

## Demo: The Cross-System Merge

Now the queries that [no single system can answer]{.amber} --- the ones that justify the enterprise software stack.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Cross-system questions**

- "Which customers buy from multiple divisions?"
- "What's revenue per employee by division?"
- "Compare our top customer's pipeline to their support ticket trends"
::::

::::{.column}
**What to watch**

- Agent queries 2--3 systems separately
- Fuzzy matching reconciles customer names
- Python merges the results
- The agent [shows its work]{.amber}: SQL + merge logic
::::
:::

This is the query that takes the BI team 3 weeks. The agent does it in [30 seconds]{.amber}.

## Demo: Full Reporting Pipeline {.section-divider}

## Demo: From Question to Board Memo

The "last mile" --- not just data, but a [delivered artifact]{.amber}.

Ask Meridian:

> *"Prepare a quarterly executive summary of Meridian Corp performance --- revenue by division, headcount efficiency, top customer risks, and supply chain issues."*

The agent will:

1. Query 6+ systems (CRM, Finance, HR, Support, Supply Chain)
2. Merge and reconcile across divisions
3. Generate charts
4. Write a structured narrative memo

This is the pipeline that [replaces the reporting team's weekly cycle]{.amber}.

## What's at Risk {.section-divider}

## The SaaS Revenue Model Under Pressure

Enterprise software charges [per seat, per month]{.amber}. AI agents threaten the math.

| Layer | Examples | Annual spend | AI threat |
|---|---|---|---|
| CRM | Salesforce, HubSpot | $150/user/mo | Query layer replaceable |
| BI / Analytics | Tableau, Power BI | $70/user/mo | [High]{.amber} --- dashboards are natural language |
| Integration | MuleSoft, Informatica | $50K--500K/yr | Agent-based merging as alternative |
| Reporting | Workiva, Adaptive | $30/user/mo | [High]{.amber} --- narrative generation |
| Support | Zendesk, ServiceNow | $100/user/mo | Tier 1 triage automatable |

The [most vulnerable]{.amber}: anything that is fundamentally a natural-language interface to structured data.

## The Counter-Argument {.section-divider}

## Why ERP Isn't Dead

The SaaSpocalypse thesis has real limits. Enterprise software does things AI agents [cannot easily replace]{.amber}.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**What ERP Does Beyond Queries**]{.card-title}

- Transaction processing (orders, invoices, payments)
- Workflow enforcement (approval chains, compliance gates)
- Audit trails with legal standing
- Role-based access control at the field level
- Real-time integrations (inventory, shipping, payments)
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**What AI Agents Can't Do (Yet)**]{.card-title}

- Process a purchase order end-to-end
- Enforce SOX compliance controls
- Guarantee data consistency across systems
- Replace the system of record
- Provide legally defensible audit trails
:::
:::

ERP is a [system of record]{.amber}. AI agents are a [system of insight]{.amber}. These are different jobs.

## The Integration Layer Is the Real Target

The $2T thesis is [partially right, partially wrong]{.amber}.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**What AI will displace**

- BI dashboards and ad-hoc reporting
- Data integration middleware (for read-only queries)
- Analyst time spent on data wrangling
- Tier 1 support triage
- Manual report generation
::::

::::{.column}
**What survives**

- Transaction processing engines
- Compliance and audit infrastructure
- Workflow orchestration with legal standing
- Master data management
- Systems of record
::::
:::

The disruption isn't "AI replaces SAP." It's ["AI replaces the $200K/year you spend making SAP's data accessible."]{.amber}

## What This Means for Finance Professionals

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Near Term (2026--2027)**]{.card-title}

- AI agents supplement BI tools, not replace them
- Cross-system queries become self-service
- Reporting cycles compress from weeks to hours
- New role: [AI verifier]{.amber} --- checking agent output, not producing it
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Medium Term (2027--2029)**]{.card-title}

- ERP vendors embed AI agents natively (SAP Joule, Salesforce Agentforce)
- Standalone BI tools lose market share
- Data warehouse projects face "build vs. agent" decisions
- Compliance frameworks catch up
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Long Term (2029+)**]{.card-title}

- The "reporting layer" is fully AI-native
- ERP becomes invisible infrastructure (like databases today)
- Competitive advantage shifts from data access to [data judgment]{.amber}
- The CFO's question isn't "can we get the data?" --- it's "can we trust the answer?"
:::
:::

## The Verification Problem at Enterprise Scale

Everything from Module 7 applies here (and Module 13 for details). An AI agent querying 10 systems makes [all the same errors]{.amber} --- at enterprise scale.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**The demo looked impressive. But...**

- Did the fuzzy matching miss a customer?
- Did the date format mismatch skew Q4 numbers?
- Did the agent silently drop rows with NULL values?
- Did it average across won and lost deals?
::::

::::{.column}
**The verification checklist still applies**

1. Sanity-check with known answers
2. "Show me the SQL" for each system
3. Cross-check by rephrasing
4. Spot-check edge cases and merge logic

[The more systems involved, the more places for silent errors.]{.amber}
::::
:::

## {.centered-statement}

**The SaaSpocalypse isn't about whether AI can query your data.**

**It's about whether anyone will [trust the answer]{.amber} enough to act on it.**

## AI Adoption Roadmap {.section-divider}

## The Sandbox-Audit-Deploy Pattern

Before deploying any AI workflow to your team, follow three phases.

:::{.step-flow}
:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
🧪
:::
:::{.step-title}
1. Sandbox
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Test with dummy data
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
🔍
:::
:::{.step-title}
2. Audit
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Review logs and logic
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
🚀
:::
:::{.step-title}
3. Deploy
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Roll out with monitoring
:::
:::
:::

Test with synthetic data, review logs and logic, then roll out with weekly monitoring.

## Adoption Timeline: From Quick Wins to Strategic

Each adoption stage maps to a trust level.

1. **Quick wins** (weeks) --- *light verification*:
   - Chat for research, summarization, drafts
   - Upload spreadsheets for ad-hoc analysis

2. **Medium-term** (months) --- *test-before-deploy*:
   - Skills for recurring workflows
   - MCP integrations and web apps

3. **Strategic** (quarters) --- *full audit trail*:
   - Production apps with authentication
   - AI usage policy and governance framework

## Small Language Models and Fine-Tuning {.section-divider}

## Improving Reliability: Fine-Tuning and SLMs

A complementary approach to verification: make the model itself more reliable for your tasks.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Fine-tuning an LLM**

- Train on your domain data (10-Ks, memos, deal docs)
- Model learns your terminology and conventions
- Fewer silent errors on domain-specific tasks
::::

::::{.column}
**Small Language Models (SLMs)**

- ~1--8B parameters (vs. hundreds of billions)
- Fine-tuned in hours on a single GPU
- Run on-premise --- [data never leaves your network]{.amber}
- [Specialized small models can outperform large models on narrow tasks]{.amber}
::::
:::

## Why SLMs for Agentic Systems?

Belcak et al. (2025), "[Small Language Models are the Future of Agentic AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.02153)":

- Most agent subtasks are [repetitive, scoped, and non-conversational]{.amber} --- classifying documents, extracting fields, formatting outputs
- SLMs fine-tuned on a single task outperform LLMs on format compliance and consistency
- 10--30x cheaper in latency, energy, and compute vs. large models
- On-device deployment enables real-time, offline inference with full data privacy
- Agents can use [multiple models]{.amber}: an LLM for complex reasoning, SLMs for routine subtasks

## From LLM to SLM: The Conversion Process

The paper proposes a six-step migration path:

:::{.step-flow-6}
:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
📝
:::
:::{.step-title}
1. Log
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Agent interactions (prompts, responses, tool calls)
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
🧹
:::
:::{.step-title}
2. Curate
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Remove PII, filter for quality
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
🔗
:::
:::{.step-title}
3. Cluster
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Identify recurring task patterns
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
📐
:::
:::{.step-title}
4. Select
:::
:::{.step-desc}
SLM sized to each task's complexity
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
⚙️
:::
:::{.step-title}
5. Fine-tune
:::
:::{.step-desc}
LoRA with 10K--100K examples
:::
:::

:::{.step-card}
:::{.step-icon}
🔄
:::
:::{.step-title}
6. Repeat
:::
:::{.step-desc}
Retrain as usage patterns evolve
:::
:::
:::

[The audit logs from earlier in this module are exactly the data you need for step 1.]{.amber}

## Summary {.section-divider}

## Summary

:::{.four-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Guardrails**]{.card-title}

- Permissions & hooks
- CLAUDE.md constraints
- Layered defense
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Data Security**]{.card-title}

- Schema-only pattern
- Local AI / Ollama
- Data sovereignty
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**ERP & SaaSpocalypse**]{.card-title}

- $2T market signal
- System of record vs. insight
- Meridian demo
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Enterprise Adoption**]{.card-title}

- Sandbox-audit-deploy
- SLMs & fine-tuning
- Adoption roadmap
:::
:::

:::{style="text-align:center; margin-top:1em;"}
[Control the agent, protect the data, understand the disruption.]{.amber}
:::
